title: The hidden unfairness in your tax system and how to fix it
gist: New World Bank research across 25 low- and middle-income countries finds self-employed workers in the top income decile pay about 36% less tax than salaried peers with identical income because third-party withholding makes employees easy to tax while the self-employed self-report and evade; a budget-neutral shift from VAT to income tax improves progressivity across income groups but worsens this within-group inequity, and large citizen surveys show the public wants enforcement, not a different tax mix.

insights.claim: Across 25 low- and middle-income countries, a self-employed individual in the top income decile pays approximately 36% less in taxes than a salaried peer with identical income, based on micro-tax simulation models.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: A quantified, structural mispricing of tax burden by employment type — relevant to any analysis of informal-sector incentives or labor-market formalization policy in emerging markets.
insights.quote: a self-employed individual pays approximately 36 percent less in taxes than a salaried peer with identical income
insights.locator: The gap is large, and it sits at the top

insights.claim: In the top income decile of the 25 countries studied, self-employed workers make up nearly half of all earners, compared with just 10% in high-income countries.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: High-income-decile self-employment share is a proxy for how much revenue a tax authority structurally fails to capture at the top — a comparative metric across emerging-market tax systems.
insights.quote: the self-employed make up nearly half of all earners, compared to just 10 percent in high-income countries
insights.locator: The gap is large, and it sits at the top

insights.claim: Employers withhold and report employee taxes directly via third parties, making evasion difficult, while the self-employed self-report their own income, and in countries with limited enforcement capacity, underreporting is common and largely unchecked.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Identifies the enforcement mechanism (third-party reporting vs. self-reporting) as the root cause of the gap — points directly to digital invoicing and third-party data integration as the fix, not just rate changes.

themes.theme: A hidden trade-off in tax reform design

insights.claim: A budget-neutral reform that raises income tax while cutting VAT improves progressivity across income groups (the rich pay relatively more) but worsens equity within income groups, because it mostly raises the burden on salaried workers while self-employed people at the same income level continue to underreport and largely escape it.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Challenges the standard prescription that shifting from VAT to income tax is automatically fairer; reformers relying on textbook progressivity metrics may be blind to the within-group unfairness that drives political backlash.
insights.quote: The reform that looks progressive on paper can deepen unfairness in practice.
insights.locator: A sharp trade-off in tax design

insights.claim: Consumption taxes like VAT treat employees and self-employed workers more fairly than income taxes do, because people at similar income levels tend to spend their money in similar ways regardless of employment status, so the tax burden is distributed more evenly across worker types even though VAT is less progressive across income groups.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Reframes VAT as the more horizontally equitable instrument in weak-enforcement settings — a counter-intuitive input for tax-policy analysis that typically favors income tax on progressivity grounds alone.
insights.quote: consumption taxes... treat employees and self-employed workers more fairly than income taxes do
insights.locator: Here is what may be surprising

insights.claim: A survey of over 17,600 respondents across six middle-income countries, including Pakistan, found that when told the actual size of the tax gap — for instance, that salaried workers in Pakistan pay four times more income tax than self-employed individuals with similar earnings — respondents' concern about the unfairness increased sharply.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Shows the unfairness is currently under-appreciated by the public — communicating the gap's size could itself become a political lever (or obstacle) for tax reformers.
insights.quote: salaried workers in Pakistan pay four times more in income tax than self-employed individuals with similar earnings

insights.claim: Learning about the tax gap did not shift citizen preferences toward consumption taxes as a fix; instead, respondents who became more concerned concluded the government could reduce evasion if it genuinely tried, i.e., they blamed poor administration rather than the tax mix.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: A political-economy insight: there is no public constituency for a VAT-based fix — reform coalitions need to be built around visible enforcement improvements instead.
insights.quote: Their conclusion was not that the tax mix was wrong, it was that the system was being poorly administered.
insights.locator: The revealing part is that learning about the gap

insights.claim: Because citizens perceive that income-tax reforms fall disproportionately on easy-to-tax employees while the self-employed remain largely untouched, this perception becomes a political constraint that drives resistance to further income-tax-based reforms before they even start.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: The equity gap is not a fairness footnote — it is a binding political constraint on the entire tax base-broadening agenda in these countries.
insights.quote: That perception becomes a constraint on any reform process before it even starts.
insights.locator: What this means for tax reform

insights.claim: For base broadening to succeed, governments need to pair changes to tax schedules with visible, credible improvements in enforcement capacity — third-party reporting, digital invoicing, and data integration between revenue authorities and financial institutions.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: A concrete policy checklist for judging whether a country's tax-reform package is likely to be politically durable versus likely to stall on legitimacy grounds.
insights.quote: third-party reporting, digital invoicing, and data integration between revenue authorities and financial institutions are the mechanisms through which equity across worker types can be improved in practice

insights.claim: The same horizontal-equity logic extends beyond the employee/self-employed divide: international tax avoidance and the preferential treatment of capital income over labor income generate similar inequities and similar political resistance to reform.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Generalizes the finding into a framework applicable to global tax-avoidance and capital-vs-labor taxation debates — relevant for international tax policy and capital-gains-rate discussions.
insights.locator: The paper also notes that the same logic applies
